coredata.dc:title: A simple fluorescent “Turn off-on” sensor based on P, N-doped graphene quantum dots for Hg2+ and Cysteine determination
coredata.dc:creator: Şenol, Ayşe Merve
Kassa, Solomon Bezabeh
Onganer, Yavuz
coredata.dc:description: A new dual-doped, Phosphorus(P) and Nitrogen(N), fluorescent graphene quantum dots (P, N-GQDs) have been synthesized and used to detect “Mercury(II)-Cysteine (Hg2+-Cys)” as a “Turn off-on” fluorescent sensor. First, blue-green emitting P, N-GQDs with high quantum efficiency were successfully prepared for the first time from lemon salt by thermal pyrolysis method, which is simple and fast. The structure of P, N-GQDs was analyzed by the spectroscopic techniques. Next, P, N-GQDs were examined as a "Turn-off" fluorescent sensor to detect various metal ions. It was determined that the fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs was selectivity quenched only with Hg2+ ions. Afterwards, Hg2+ loaded P, N-GQDs (P, N-GQDs@Hg) were studied as a "Turn-on" fluorescent sensor to detect various amino acids/biomolecules. The fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs@Hg was determined to be almost completely (about 95%) and selectively recovered with cysteine. Thus, it was determined that P, N-GQDs could be used selectively and sensitively as "Turn off-on" fluorescence sensors for “Hg2+-Cys”. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.13 µM for Hg2+ and 0.65 µM for Cys and the interference experiments were carried out for each ion and molecule. In addition, the designed method was successfully applied to the real samples. Consequently, the P, N-GQDs could be applied as a very precise and selective "Turn-off-on" fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ and Cys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorus(P) and nitrogen(N) doped graphene quantum dots (P, N-GQDs)
Mercury ions
Cysteine
Fluoroscence sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of P, N-GQDs by thermal pyrolysis method
Fluorescence responses of cations and amino acids/biomolecules
Detection of Hg2+ and Cysteine in the real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research has been supported by Atatürk University , Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit, BAP. (Grant Number: FBA-2021–8808 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram for the syntheses of P, N-GQDs.
Absorption spectrum of P, N-GQDs in deionized water.

Fluorescence spectra of P, N-GQDs in deionized water at the excitation wavelength (a) 310–410 nm (“up-conversion” method), (b) 420–490 nm (“up-conversion” method), and (c) 640–850 nm (“down-conversion” method).
Fluorescence spectra of P, N-GQDs containing various metal cations (164 µM) in PBS (λex=350 nm).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of P, N-GQDs containing different Hg2+ concentrations (1.59–136.9 µM), plot of F0/F vs. Hg2+ concentrations in the range of (b) 1.59–136.9 µM, and (c) 1.59–16.3 µM (λex= 350 nm).
Histograms of their relative fluorescence intensities for competing metal cations in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions (λex=350 nm).
Fluorescence spectra of P, N-GQDs, P, N-GQDs@Hg, P, N-GQDs@Hg containing various amino acids/biomolecules (224 µM) in PBS (λex=350 nm).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of P, N-GQDs@Hg containing different Cys concentrations (3.32–228.0 µM), plot of F/F0 vs. Cys various concentrations in the range of (b) 3.32–228.0 µM, and (c) 3.32–38.5 µM (λex = 350 nm).
Histograms for amino acids/biomolecules (0.164 mM) and P, N-GQDs@Hg containing system in the absence and presence of Cys (0.224 mM). A= Amino acids/biomolecules (λex=350 nm).
Some fluorescent probes and LOD values that can be used to detect Hg2+ and Cys.

head.ce:title: A simple fluorescent “Turn off-on” sensor based on P, N-doped graphene quantum dots for Hg2+ and Cysteine determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Atatürk University, Erzurum 25240, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Atatürk University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Atatürk University, Erzurum 25240, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Program of Medical Laboratory Techniques, Department of Medical Services and Techniques, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul 34668, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Program of Medical Laboratory Techniques, Department of Medical Services and Techniques, University of Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Program of Medical Laboratory Techniques, Department of Medical Services and Techniques, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul 34668, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new dual-doped, Phosphorus(P) and Nitrogen(N), fluorescent graphene quantum dots (P, N-GQDs) have been synthesized and used to detect “Mercury(II)-Cysteine (Hg2+-Cys)” as a “Turn off-on” fluorescent sensor. First, blue-green emitting P, N-GQDs with high quantum efficiency were successfully prepared for the first time from lemon salt by thermal pyrolysis method, which is simple and fast. The structure of P, N-GQDs was analyzed by the spectroscopic techniques. Next, P, N-GQDs were examined as a "Turn-off" fluorescent sensor to detect various metal ions. It was determined that the fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs was selectivity quenched only with Hg2+ ions. Afterwards, Hg2+ loaded P, N-GQDs (P, N-GQDs@Hg) were studied as a "Turn-on" fluorescent sensor to detect various amino acids/biomolecules. The fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs@Hg was determined to be almost completely (about 95%) and selectively recovered with cysteine. Thus, it was determined that P, N-GQDs could be used selectively and sensitively as "Turn off-on" fluorescence sensors for “Hg2+-Cys”. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.13 µM for Hg2+ and 0.65 µM for Cys and the interference experiments were carried out for each ion and molecule. In addition, the designed method was successfully applied to the real samples. Consequently, the P, N-GQDs could be applied as a very precise and selective "Turn-off-on" fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ and Cys.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Phosphorus(P) and nitrogen(N) doped graphene quantum dots (P, N-GQDs)

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) as compared with conventional fluorescent substances (organic dyes, polymer conjugates, etc.) are used in many applications since they have significant advantages such as strong absorption, high photostability, high fluorescence quantum yield, and large Stokes shift [1,2]. However, the heavy metal content of most semiconductor QDs (CdHgTe, CdTe@CdSe, CdTeSe@CdZnS, PbS, etc.) have limited biological applications. They cause biological and environmental hazards [3]. Also, semiconductor QDs are toxic and have very low solubility. Due to these disadvantages of QDs, many alternative fluorophores (fluorescent substances) are synthesized for bioimaging, sensors, drug delivery systems, and optical applications [4,5].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are environmentally friendly nanomaterials that outperform semiconductor QDs, are one of the most attractive fluorescent materials in recent years. GQDs are usually 2–20 nm in size and are crystalline zero-dimensional disks of graphene [6]. GQDs have outstanding properties such as tunable fluorescence, low toxicity, high water solubility, high biocompatibility, large surface area, easy modification and functionalization, excellent optical and chemical stability. Thanks to these advantages, it is widely used in many fields such as biological imaging, optical sensing, photocatalytic degradation, solar cells, light emitting diodes, drug delivery systems, biomedical cancer therapies and sensors [4,7,8].

The heteroatom doping (F, P, N, S, O, B, etc.) of GQDs is shown to change their chemical, electronic and optical properties [9–11]. Nitrogen (N) is one of the most suitable heteroatoms for doping because of its five valence electrons in the structure and its size close to carbon atoms [12]. Phosphorus atoms (P) having five valence electrons in the structure are bigger than carbon atoms. Therefore, they can create substitutional flaws in the carbon cluster by acting as n-type donors and change GQDs’ electrical and optical characteristics [13]. In addition, it is determined to improve properties, especially fluorescence quantum yields and electronic properties of GQDs by doping with more than one heteroatom in recent studies [6,14].

There are two types of approaches for synthesizing GQDs, bottom-up and top-down. In the top-down synthesis method, nano-sized GQDs are obtained from large-mass carbon material in various ways. This synthesis facilitates the solubilization and functionalization of GQDs by producing oxygen-containing functional groups as well as providing the advantage of abundant raw materials. However, it has some disadvantages such as low yield, uncontrollable size and shape. In the bottom-up synthesis method, GQDs are synthesized by using suitable molecules such as small molecules and polymers as initiators with hydrothermal, microwave-assisted hydrothermal and soft-template or metal-catalyzed methods. Unlike top-down synthesis, bottom-up synthesis is less imperfect and size and morphology can be controlled. For example, GQDs are produced by using top-down methods from carbonic initiators such as graphite, coal, graphene oxide (GO)/H2O2 under UV light, GO/dimethyl formamide (DMF), graphite derivative or bottom-up methods from initiators such as hexabromobenzene/methylbenzene, citric acid, norepinephrine, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and adenisine triphospahte (ATP). However, strong oxidizers (sulfuric acid, nitric acid etc.) and complex processes (chemical oxidation, solvothermal etc.) are used in GQDs syntheses from carbonic initiators such as graphite and coal. These syntheses have many disadvantages such as the release of toxic gases, complex and long synthesis process, high cost, difficult and long removal of acid residues and/or metallic impurities. For these reasons, it is necessary to develop environmentally and friendly alternative methods to find new environmental initiators. In recent years, GQDs have been synthesized using pyrolysis carbonization and/or hydrothermal method, which is an effective and environmentally and friendly method [15,16].

Mercury ions (Hg2+) are neurotoxic heavy metal, which can be found widely in water, soil, and foodstuffs. Hg2+, which is very active against amino and thiol groups in nucleic acids and peptides, can cause serious damage to the kidneys, liver, and brain by accumulating in the human body through the food chain, as well as being an important risk in the environmental, biological and chemical systems [17,18].

Cysteine (Cys), a thiol derivative amino acid with a low molecular weight, plays an essential role in the vital activities of living things. Cysteine excess causes diseases such as neurotoxicity and schizophrenia while cysteine deficiency causes skin lesions, liver damage, growth retardation, fatigue, weakness and etc. [17,19]. Amino acids having similar features coexist in many food and biological samples. Cysteine is generally determined by HPLC and capillary electrophoresis methods [20,21]. However, these methods are costly because of advanced equipment and also difficult to prepare samples [22]. Therefore, it is very important to determine Hg2+ and Cys by a simple, fast, and sensitive method.

In recent years, fluorescence sensors have been developed as a powerful method for detecting analytes due to their sensitivity, simplicity and convenience [23]. In the literature, the syntheses of some GQDs derivatives and their ion and/or substance detection as fluorescence sensors are given in Table S1. However, studies on dual fluorescent systems such as Hg2+ and cysteine determination are still quite limited and development would be helpful. [17,18,24].

In this study, we proved that P, N-GQDs can be used as “Turn off - on” fluorescence sensors to detect “Hg2+ - Cys” in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution. First of all, blue-green emitting P, N-GQDs with high quantum efficiency were prepared for the first time from lemon salt by thermal pyrolysis method in the presence of glycine, diammonium phosphate (DAP) and phosphoric acid. As-prepared P, N-GQDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and UV-Vis absorption and steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. In addition, to determine the fluorescence stability of P, N-GQDs, their fluorescence properties at various pHs, various salt concentrations and UV light exposure time were investigated. Secondly, the P, N-GQDs were examined as a "Turn-off" fluorescent sensor to detect Hg2+ ions. Thirdly, Hg2+ loaded P, N-GQDs (P, N-GQDs@Hg) were studied as a "Turn-on" fluorescent sensor to detect Cysteine. Furthermore, the LOD values were determined and their interference experiments were made for each ion/molecule. Afterwards, P, N-GQDs and P, N-GQDs@Hg were used to detect Hg2+ and Cys in real water samples (natural spring water and river water) and the serum sample, respectively.

Glycine, phosphoric acid and diammonium phosphate (DAP) used in the synthesis and rhodamine 6 G (R6G), quinine sulfate (QS) exploted for the determination of relative quantum yields (Φf) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Lemon salt, which was used for the first time as a carbon source to prepare the P, N-GQDs, was purchased from the local supermarket. Metal cations (Ni(NO3)2.6H2O, KCl, MnCl2.4H2O, LiCl, NaCl, BaCl2.2H2O, AlCl3, CaCl2.2H2O, ZnCl2, MgCl2.6H2O, Pb(CH3COO)2.3H2O, CoCl2.6H2O, CuCl2.2H2O, FeSO4.7H2O, CrCl3.6H2O, CdCl2, HgCl2, FeCl3) and L-Cysteine (Cys), L-Lysine (Lys), DL-Proline (Pro), Ascorbic acid (AA), Glucose (Glu), Glycine (Gly) from Sigma Aldrich; L-Serine (Ser), L-Alanine (Ala) from BLD Pharm; L-Valine (Val), L-Methionine (Met), L-Threonine (Thr), and L-Arginine (Arg) from Carl Roth; L-Leucine (Leu) and L-Isoleucine (Ile) from TCI; Glutathione (GSH) from Bioshop and L-(+)-Aspartic acid (Asp A) from Acros Organics were used in fluorescence sensor studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of P, N-GQDs by thermal pyrolysis method
ce:section.ce:para: Lemon salt was used for the first time as a new initiator to synthesize P, N-GQDs. 0.5 g lemon salt was mixed homogeneously in a beaker with 0.2 g glycine, 0.4 g DAP and 0.4 ml phosphoric acid. The mixture was heated at 225 °C for 10 min. It was observed that the mixture melted in 3 min and its color changed from colorless to dark brown. This indicated that blue/green fluorescent P, N-GQDs were synthesized. The P, N-GQDs were cooled to room temperature. The obtained P, N-GQDs were dissolved by adding 10 ml of deionized water. The pH of the prepared solution was adjusted to 7.0 by using 0.1 M NaOH and centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles in the solution. The remaining supernatant solution was passed through 0.45 and 0.22 µm filters. The P, N-GQD solution was diluted to 50 ml by deionized water for final experiments and stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator for applications [25]. The concentration of the solution is ∼ 10 mg/ml. A representative scheme for the syntheses of P, N-GQDs is given in Fig. 1.

TEM images were taken in an FEI TALOS F200S TEM 200 kV. XPS analysis was explored by Spect‐Flex including a standard Al X‐ray source. XRD analysis was recorded by Rigaku Miniflex diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (30 kV, 15 mA, λ = 1.54051 Å). FT-IR spectrum was performed by a Bruker Alpha ATR spectrometer. The absorption spectra were taken by a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra studies were done by a Shimadzu RF-5301PC Spectrofluorophotometer.

The fluorescence quantum yields (Φf) of the prepared P, N-GQDs were calculated using the previously successfully applied Parker-Rees method [26–28]. To determine the relative Φf values of P, N-GQDs with blue and green fluorescence, the quinine sulfate in 0.5 M H2SO4 (Φf=0.546) and rhodamine 6 G in ethanol (Φf=0.94) were used as references, respectively [29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of cations and amino acids/biomolecules
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a stock solution (1.0 × 10−2 M) for each substance was prepared to detect cations and amino acids/biomolecules. These stock solutions were used by diluting in the required proportions. Secondly, a stock solution (5 µg/ml) of P, N-GQDs was prepared in phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS) at pH 7 to investigate fluorescence responses of P, N-GQDs for metal cations. Then, a required proprotion of cation solution was added to 3.0 ml of this stock solution. The solution was mixed and, then, the fluorescence and absorption spectra were taken immediately. This process was repeated for each cation solution samples. The cation effect, at which the fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs almost completely quenched, was determined. Then, the same amounts of amino acids/biomolecules were added separately to the P, N-GQDs loaded with this metal cation. Each solution was mixed thoroughly and their spectra were obtained instantly. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Dual light emitting P, N-GQDs were prepared from lemon salt at 225 °C by a one-step thermal pyrolysis method in the presence of glycine and DAP/phosphoric acid. Optical characterization of prepared P, N-GQDs was performed by absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy and given in
Figs. 2 and 3, respectively.

The three characteristic absorption bands of P, N-GQDs are seen in Fig. 2. The wavelength of maximum absorption (λabs) at 235 nm is because of π-π * transitions of CC bonds in the sp3 carbon matrix in aromatic structure. The absorption band at 339 nm is due to n-π * transitions of CO and/or CN, while the broad band at 430–500 nm is usually due to surface amino groups or n-π * transitions of P = O [31,32].

The fluorescence properties of P, N-GQDs in deionized water were investigated at various fluorescence excitation wavelengths (λex). Fig. 3a shows the obtained fluorescence spectra at various λex (by the "up-conversion" method) in the range of 310–490 nm. The fluorescence intensity increased and fluorescence maximum wavelength (λflu) slightly red-shifted when λex changed from 310 nm to 350 nm for P, N-GQDs. Then, the fluorescence intensity decreased and λflu red-shifted by changing from 350 nm to 410 nm of λex. Therefore, the optimal λex for P, N-GQDs was determined as 350 nm. λflu is 434 nm when the P, N-GQDs solution is excited at 350 nm. At this condition, the P, N-GQDs solution shows blue fluorescence. When the λex of P, N-GQDs were examined in the range of 380–410 nm, it was determined that both blue and green fluorescence were observed. Furthermore, they show only green fluorescence in λex ranging from 420 to 490 nm (Fig. 3b). Considering Fig. 3b, the λex for P, N-GQDs with green fluorescence was determined as 470 nm. The λflu of P, N-GQDs excited at 470 nm is ∼519 nm. In Figs. 3a and 3b, the fluorescence properties of P, N-GQDs were studied by the up-conversion method based on two-photon absorption. To investigate the applicability of P, N-GQDs at longer wavelengths (in near infrared region, NIR, etc.), fluorescence spectra were also studied at excitation wavelengths in the range of 640–850 nm using the "down-conversion" method (Fig. 3c). It is found that the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3c are quite similar to those in Fig. 3a. λflu is 434 nm at λex 350 nm with the “up-conversion” method of P, N-GQDs in Fig. 3a while λflu is 434 nm at λex 700 nm with their “down-conversion” method in Fig. 3c. One can see that P, N-GQDs have nearly the same wavelength for obtained fluorescence spectra by the "up-conversion" method as compared to the "down-conversion" method. As a result, it has been proven that P, N-GQDs can also be studied with longer wavelengths [33]. Also, a photograph of P, N-GQDs in deionized water obtained under UV light at λex of (a) 350 nm and (b) 470 nm is given in Fig. S1. Φf values of P, N-GQDs with blue and green fluorescence were calculated as Φf = 0.30 and Φf = 0.17 at excitation wavelengths of 350 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The Φf value of P, N-GQDs compared to the GQDs and their derivatives in the literature is pretty good as given in Table S2 [34,35]. In addition, the research on P, N-GQDs are very limited in the literature [36].

The stability of P, N-GQDs at various pH values, salt concentrations and UV light exposure time was also examined by fluorescence spectrum studies and evaluated with the F/F0 parameter. Here, F and F0 are fluorescence intensity of λflu at λex = 350 nm at varying conditions and constant conditions, respectively. Namely, F/F0 represents the relative fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence spectra of P, N-GQDs were obtained in various buffer solutions (pH 3.0–11.0) over a wide pH range. The F/F0 vs. pH plot is given in Fig. S2a. It was observed that P, N-GQDs are stable over a wide pH range and show maximum at pH 8 for relative fluorescence intensity [35]. Furthermore, the effect of various salt concentrations on the P, N-GQDs’ fluorescence intensity was investigated in the range of 0.2–2.0 M. The fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs in deionized water did not change significantly with the addition of salt up to 1.0 M but their fluorescence intensity decreased slightly with the further increase in salt concentration (Fig. S2b). In addition, P, N-GQDs in deionized water are largely preserved their fluorescence intensity when exposed to UV-A light (315–400 nm) for 120 min (Fig. S2c). In general, when the fluorescence stability spectra are examined, it is concluded that the P, N-GQDs are quite stable.

Structural characterization of the P, N-GQDs was performed by TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy. TEM is one of the best methods to observe the shapes, sizes, and homogeneity of P, N-GQDs. TEM image of P, N-GQDs is given in Fig. S3a. It has been determined that the thin P, N-GQDs, which is a particle size in the range of generally ∼ 10.0 – 20.0 nm, are spherical shaped. Furthermore, XRD diffractogram was taken to determine the crystalline structure of P, N-GQDs (Fig. S3b). In Fig. S3b, the two basic peaks at 2θ ∼ 25.2° and 2θ ∼ 44.8° show (002) and (100) planes, respectively. These peaks are due to carbonic structures. The peak at 2θ ∼25.2° is related in well-organized ordered layer carbon structure. This wide peak at ∼15–50° indicated the presence of nanostructures [12,37]. Moreover, the functional groups on the P, N-GQDs’ surface are determined using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S3c). The broad bands seen in the FT-IR spectrum of P, N-GQDs at nearly 3205 cm−1 and 3029 cm−1 are due to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. The bands at 2957 and 2845 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrations of C-H groups in alkyl groups such as -CH3 and -CH2. The sharp band at 1697 cm−1 is caused by CO stretching vibrations in CONH. The sharp band at 1417 cm−1 is related to CC stretching vibrations. The band at 1324 cm−1 is due to the deformation of the C-H, O-H, and C-N groups, and the band at 1230 cm−1 is because of C-N stretching vibrations. Bands around 1175 cm−1 and 994 cm−1 are related to C-O, C-O-C, and C-P bonds. The peaks in the range of 950–1160 cm−1 correspond to the classical stretching vibrations between P-O [38–40].

Also, the P,N-GQDs’ surface was analyzed using XPS. XPS data for P, N-GQDs are given in Fig. S4. The peaks at 130.71, 284.4, 397.9, and 528.8 eV in Fig. S4a belong to the characteristic P2p, C1 s, N1 s, and O1 s, respectively, and show that P, N-GQDs are composed of phosphorus (P), carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O). In the C1 s high-resolution spectrum of P, N-GQDs (Fig. S4b), it is determined that there are six peaks belonging to the C-H (281.5 eV), C-C (282.7 eV), CC (283.9 eV), C-N/C-P (285.6 eV), C-O (286.32 eV) and CO (286.96 eV). Similarly, four peaks at 397.67 eV, 398.75 eV, 399.89 eV (C-N-C) and 401.22 eV (C-NH2) in the N1 s high-resolution spectrum of P, N-GQDs given in Fig. S4c. These peaks are due to C–N = C groups, pyridinic N, pyrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen in the structure, respectively [31]. In the P2p high-resolution spectrum of P, N-GQDs, two peaks appeared at 131.3 eV (P-C) and 133.4 eV(P-O) as seen in Fig. S4d. For the O1s high-resolution spectrum of P, N-GQDs, two peaks are seen in 529.7 eV(CO) and 531.0 eV(C-O) [41,42].

The results obtained are in agreement with each other and confirm the formation of P, N-GQDs. In addition, it is determined that the surface of P, N-GQDs is consisted of P, C, N, O, and H elements and their functional groups. These functional groups provide both stability and hydrophilicity to P, N-GQDs [43].

The fluorescence responses of P, N-GQDs in PBS (pH 7) were studied by adding eighteen different metal cations (K+, Li+, Na+, Ca+2, Ba+2, Mg+2, Mn+2, Zn+2, Cd+2, Cu+2, Co2+, Ni+2, Pb+2, Cr+3, Al+3, Fe+2, Fe+3 ve Hg+2) to determine the selectivity of P, N-GQDs. For this, firstly, a stock solution (5 µg/ml) of P, N-GQDs was prepared in PBS. A certain amount of this stock solution was taken in a flacon. Next, a determined amount of metal cation was added to the solution. This process was done separately for each metal cation (164 µM) and the fluorescence and absorption spectra were immediately taken. All fluorescence measurements were recorded in the wavelength range of 355–650 nm by adjusting λex 350 nm (Slit Intervals High Ex:3 nm-Em:5 nm). Their fluorescence spectra are given in Fig. 4.

Also, their absorption spectra and photograph under UV light are seen in Fig. S5a and Fig. S5b, respectively. In Fig. S5a, the absorbance of P, N-GQDs loaded with Hg2+ (P, N-GQD@Hg) decreased significantly and the λabs red-shifted ∼ 3 nm by widening. There was no significant change in the absorption spectra of P, N-GQDs loaded with other metal cations. This indicates a strong interaction of Hg2+ with P, N-GQDs. Consistently, in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs is seen to be quenched effectively and selectively with only Hg+2 ions without being affected by other metal cations. This fluoresence quenching is due to the effective electron transport between P, N-GQDs and Hg2+ ions. As a result, a non-fluorescent complex is formed in aqueous PBS [17,22]. Picture of UV light of P, N-GQDs containing various metal cations in PBS is consistent with these results (Fig. S5b).

To determine the sensitivity of P, N-GQDs to Hg2+ ions, the fluorescence and absorption spectra of P, N-GQDs were taken with different Hg2+ concentrations (1.59–136.9 µM). The fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 5a) and absorbance (Fig. S6) of P, N-GQDs were decreased with increasing Hg2+ concentration [17].

The quenching efficiency of P, N-GQDs with Hg2+ was evaluated using the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1). The Stern-Volmer equation as follows.
(1) F0/F = 1+ K
SV [Q]

In Eq. (1), KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of Hg2+ ions (quencher), and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 433 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions, respectively.

It is seen that the relationship between [Hg2+] and F0/F is parabolic for the P, N-GQDs@Hg system containing Hg2+ in the range of 1.59–136.9 µM (Fig. 5b). On the other hand, the Hg2+ concentration range (1.59–16.3 µM) indicates linear relationship with F0/F, therefore, the plot of [Hg2+] vs. F0/F was given in Fig. 5c. The LOD value for Hg2+ was calculated as 0.13 µM, using the 3s/k equation. In this equation, s; is the standard deviation from the blank sample and k; is the slope of plot. From the slope of Fig. 5c, KSV was found to be ∼ 2.30 × 104 M−1.

The applicability of a fluorescent sensor is decided by its ability to retain sensitivity alongside other metal cations. Therefore, the operability of the P, N-GQDs system as a Hg2+ fluorescence sensor was tested by the presence of other metal cations. For this aim, a certain amount of the P, N-GQDs solution was taken and,then, a certain amount of metal cation solution was added to it. Afterwards, Hg2+ was added to this solution. Fluorescence spectra of each solution were recorded. F/F0 values were calculated by using the λflu of P, N-GQDs, cation/ P, N-GQDs and Hg2+ loaded cation/P, N-GQDs solutions. The relative fluorescence intensity histograms for interfering metal cations in the absence and presence of Hg2+ are given in Fig. 6. When the interference histograms were evaluated, it was determined that the P, N-GQDs system could detect Hg2+ ions without being affected by the presence of other metal cations.

The fluorescence recovery experiments, various biomolecules/amino acid molecules were used to restore quenched fluorescence intensity of the P, N-GQDs@Hg (Hg+2 ion-loaded P,N-GQDs) solutions. These are Ser, Ala, Val, Met, Gly, IIe, Leu, Lys, Arg, Pro, Thr, Glu, Asp A, AA, GSH, and Cys. For the experiments, a P, N-GQDs stock solution of 5 µg/ml was firstly prepared in PBS buffer and a certain amount of Hg2+ was added to this stock solution. The resulting P, N-GQDs@Hg system was prepared immediately before each amino acid/biomolecule was added. Afterwards, the effect of each molecules on the fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs@Hg was determined. This process was performed separately for 16 different molecules and fluorescence and absorption spectra were obtained, respectively ( Fig. 7 and Fig. S7a). When Fig. 7 was examined, it was determined that the fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs@Hg was almost completely (about 95%) selectively recovered (enriched in fluorescence) with Cysteine (Cys) molecules. This is thought to be because of the strong interaction between P, N-GQDs@Hg and Cys, in other words, the high affinity between Hg2+ and sulfur atoms (S) in the structure of Cys [44]. Therefore, a strong complex is formed between Hg2+ and Cys and, therefore, the fluorescence of P, N-GQDs@Hg is largely restored [22]. Consistently, the absorbance of P, N-GQDs@Hg increased significantly and approached those of P, N-GQDs (Fig. S7a). Also, picture of UV light of P, N-GQDs@Hg containing various molecules in PBS is consistent with these results (Fig. S7b).

To determine the sensitivity of P, N-GQDs@Hg to cysteine, fluorescence spectra of P, N-GQDs@Hg at different Cys concentrations (3.32–228.0 µM) were recorded ( Fig. 8a). The fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs@Hg is seen to be increased with increasing Cys concentration. Also, absorption spectra are consistent with these data and the absorbance were generally increased with increasing Cys concentration (Fig. S8). The recovered fluorescence intensity of P, N-GQDs@Hg by the addition of Cys was analyzed by using fluorescence intensity ratios. It is seen that the relationship between [Cys] and F/F0 is parabolic in the P, N-GQDs@Hg system containing [Cys] in the range of 3.32–228.00 µM (Fig. 8b). For this reason, the Cys concentration range in which this relationship was linear was determined as 3.32–38.5 µM (Fig. 8c).

The LOD value for Cys was found as 0.65 µM. KSV was determined as 1.07 × 104 M−1. Additionally, the P, N-GQDs@Hg systems’ selectivity for Cys detection was assessed in the presence of interfering amino acids/biomolecules. Each amino acid/biomolecules was separately added to the P, N-GQDs@Hg in PBS and, then, Cys was added each of these systems. The fluorescence spectra of these solutions were recorded. The relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0 values) were calculated by using the fluorescence peak intensities at maxima of the P, N-GQDs, P, N-GQDs@Hg and P, N-GQDs@Hg loaded molecule solutions. Histograms in the absence and presence of Cys for amino acid/biomolecule are given in Fig. 9. When the interference histograms were evaluated, it was concluded that the P, N-GQDs@Hg system could detect Cys without being affected by the presence of other amino acids or biomolecules.

As a result, it was determined that easily and quickly prepared a novel P, N-GQDs can apply as a sensitive and selective "Turn off-on" fluorescent sensor for Hg+2 and Cys. In the literature, the number of fluorescent probes that can detect both Hg2+ and Cys is quite limited ( Table 1) and any fluorescence sensor that detects Hg2+ and/or Cys made with P, N-GQDs have not been encountered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and Cysteine in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For this aim, the P, N-GQDs to determine Hg2+ was investigated in real water samples (natural spring and river water). P, N-GQDs including various amounts of Hg2+ ions (10, 20, and 30 μM) were independently added to the tap and natural water samples. Each sample was analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy and the results are summarized in the Table S3. Then, the using of P, N-GQDs@Hg to determine Cys was investigated in serum sample. After adding a specific amount of Hg2+ to the blood serum was added separately specific concentrations (43, 55, and 81 μM) of Cys. Next, it was analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy. Experimental data and calculated data are given in Table S4. The results (in Tables S3 and S4) showed that the recoveries were between 96.4% and 101.9%. At the same time, the relative standard deviation (RSD) values are also quite low. Thus, it was proven that P, N-GQDs can also be used for Hg2+ and Cys detection in real samples.

Dual light emitting P, N-GQDs were prepared for the first time with lemon salt at 225 °C by a one-step thermal pyrolysis method in the presence of glycine and DAP/phosphoric acid. Firstly, P, N-GQDs were characterized spectroscopically. The fluorescence studies showed that P, N-GQDs can also be studied with a quite wider wavelength range of 310–850 nm. Furthermore, Φf values of P, N-GQDs with blue and green fluorescence were calculated as Φf= 0.30 and Φf= 0.17 at the excitation wavelengths of 350 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The absorption spectrum also confirmed the formation of P, N-GQDs. Then, P, N-GQDs were structurally characterized by TEM, XPS, XRD and FT-IR techniques. The obtained results, together with the spectroscopic data, proved successfully preparation of P, N-GQDs. Moreover, P, N-GQDs at various pH values, salt concentrations and UV light exposure time were determined to be stable. A novel P, N-GQDs prepared by simply and fastly were determined to be use as a selective and precisive "Turn off-on" fluorescent sensor for Hg+2 and Cys with the LOD of 0.13 and 0.65 µM, respectively. In addition, P, N-GQDs in PBS can be successfully applied to real samples. Considering all the advantages, including low cost, quickness, simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, this innovative fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ and Cys determination proposes a very distinctive and promising alternative.

Ayşe Merve Şenol: Visualization, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Solomon Bezabeh Kassa: Investigation, Methodology. Yavuz Onganer: Resources, Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been supported by Atatürk University, Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit, BAP. (Grant Number: FBA-2021–8808).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.sna.2023.114362.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ayşe Merve Şenol: is an Asst. Professor in Program of Medical Laboratory Techniques, Department of Medical Services and Techniques, University of Health Sciences, İstanbul, Türkiye. She received her MSc and Ph.D. from Department of Chemistry, Atatürk University, Türkiye. Her current interests are fluorescent materials, nanomaterials, optical sensor and graphene nanocomposites.
Solomon Bezabeh Kassa: is received Ph.D. from Department of Chemistry, Atatürk University, Türkiye. His current interests are fluorescent materials and optical sensor.

Yavuz Onganer: is currently a professor of Faculty of Science at Atatürk University, Türkiye. He received his Ph.D. in physical chemistry from chemistry and biochemistry department of Texas Tech University in 1993. His current research interests include photophysics and sensor features of fluorescent compounds/carbon dots or carbon quantum dots.
